lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Here, stand behind this bulk; straight will he come:

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Wear thy good rapier bare, and put it home:

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Quick, quick; fear nothing; I’ll be at thy elbow:

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: It makes us, or it mars us; think on that,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: And fix most firm thy resolution.

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: Be near at hand; I may miscarry in’t.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Here, at thy hand: be bold, and take thy stand.

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: I have no great devotion to the deed;

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: And yet he hath given me satisfying reasons:

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: ’Tis but a man gone. Forth, my sword: he dies.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: I have rubb’d this young quat almost to the sense,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: And he grows angry. Now, whether he kill Cassio,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Or Cassio him, or each do kill the other,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Every way makes my gain: live Roderigo,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: He calls me to a restitution large

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Of gold and jewels that I bobb’d from him,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: As gifts to Desdemona;

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: It must not be: if Cassio do remain,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: He hath a daily beauty in his life

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: That makes me ugly; and, besides, the Moor

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: May unfold me to him; there stand I in much peril:

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: No, he must die. But so: I hear him coming.

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: I know his gait, ’tis he.—Villain, thou diest! Makes a pass at Cassio.

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: That thrust had been mine enemy indeed,

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: But that my coat is better than thou know’st

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: I will make proof of thine.

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: O, I am slain! Iago from behind wounds Cassio in the leg, and exit.

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: I am maim’d for ever. Help, ho! murder! murder!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: The voice of Cassio: Iago keeps his word.

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: O, villain that I am!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: It is even so.

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: O, help, ho! light! a surgeon!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: ’Tis he:—O brave Iago, honest and just,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: That hast such noble sense of thy friend’s wrong!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Thou teachest me. Minion, your dear lies dead,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: And your unblest fate hies: strumpet, I come.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Forth of my heart those charms, thine eyes, are blotted;

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Thy bed, lust-stain’d, shall with lust’s blood be spotted.

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: What, ho! no watch? no passage? murder! murder!

lines.speaker: GRATIANO
lines.text: ’Tis some mischance; the cry is very direful.

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: O, help!

lines.speaker: LODOVICO
lines.text: Hark!

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: O wretched villain!

lines.speaker: LODOVICO
lines.text: Two or three groan: it is a heavy night:

lines.speaker: LODOVICO
lines.text: These may be counterfeits: let’s think’t unsafe

lines.speaker: LODOVICO
lines.text: To come in to the cry without more help.

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: Nobody come? then shall I bleed to death.

lines.speaker: LODOVICO
lines.text: Hark!

lines.speaker: GRATIANO
lines.text: Here’s one comes in his shirt, with light and weapons.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Who’s there? whose noise is this that ones on murder?

lines.speaker: LODOVICO
lines.text: We do not know.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Did not you hear a cry?

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Here, here! for heaven’s sake, help me!

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: What’s the matter?

lines.speaker: GRATIANO
lines.text: This is Othello’s ancient, as I take it.

lines.speaker: LODOVICO
lines.text: The same indeed; a very valiant fellow.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: What are you here that cry so grievously?

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Iago? O, I am spoil’d, undone by villains!

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Give me some help.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: O me, lieutenant! what villains have done this?

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: I think that one of them is hereabout,

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: And cannot make away.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: O treacherous villains!

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: What are you there? come in, and give some help. To Lodovico and Gratiano.

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: O, help me here!

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: That’s one of them.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: O murderous slave! O villain!

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: O damn’d Iago! O inhuman dog!

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Kill men i’ the dark!—Where be these bloody thieves?—

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: How silent is this town!—Ho! murder! murder!—

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: What may you be? are you of good or evil?

lines.speaker: LODOVICO
lines.text: As you shall prove us, praise us.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Signior Lodovico?

lines.speaker: LODOVICO
lines.text: He, sir.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: I cry you mercy. Here’s Cassio hurt by villains.

lines.speaker: GRATIANO
lines.text: Cassio!

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: How is’t, brother!

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: My leg is cut in two.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Marry, heaven forbid!

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Light, gentlemen; I’ll bind it with my shirt.

lines.speaker: BIANCA
lines.text: What is the matter, ho? who is’t that cried?

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Who is’t that cried!

lines.speaker: BIANCA
lines.text: O my dear Cassio! my sweet Cassio! O Cassio,

lines.speaker: BIANCA
lines.text: Cassio, Cassio!

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: O notable strumpet! Cassio, may you suspect

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Who they should be that have thus many led you?

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: No.

lines.speaker: GRATIANO
lines.text: I am to find you thus: I have been to seek you.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Lend me a garter. So. O, for a chair,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: To bear him easily hence!

lines.speaker: BIANCA
lines.text: Alas, he faints! O Cassio, Cassio, Cassio!

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Gentlemen all, I do suspect this trash

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: To be a party in this injury.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Patience awhile, good Cassio. Come, come;

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Lend me a light. Know we this face or no?

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Alas my friend and my dear countryman

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Roderigo! no:—yes, sure: O heaven! Roderigo.

lines.speaker: GRATIANO
lines.text: What, of Venice?

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Even he, sir; did you know him?

lines.speaker: GRATIANO
lines.text: Know him! ay.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Signior Gratiano? I cry you gentle pardon;

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: These bloody accidents must excuse my manners,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: That so neglected you.

lines.speaker: GRATIANO
lines.text: I am glad to see you.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: How do you, Cassio? O, a chair, a chair!

lines.speaker: GRATIANO
lines.text: Roderigo!

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: He, he ’tis he. A chair brought in. O, that’s well said; the chair!

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Some good man bear him carefully from hence;

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: I’ll fetch the general’s surgeon. To Bianca. For you, mistress,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Save you your labour. He that lies slain here, Cassio,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Was my dear friend: what malice was between you?

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: None in the world; nor do I know the man.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: To Bianca. What, look you pale? O, bear him out o’ the air. Cassio and Roderigo are borne off.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Stay you, good gentlemen. Look you pale, mistress?

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Do you perceive the gastness of her eye?

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Nay, if you stare, we shall hear more anon.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Behold her well; I pray you, look upon her:

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Do you see, gentlemen? nay, guiltiness will speak,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Though tongues were out of use.

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: ’Las, what’s the matter? what’s the matter, husband?

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Cassio hath here been set on in the dark

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: By Roderigo and fellows that are scaped:

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: He’s almost slain, and Roderigo dead.

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Alas, good gentleman! alas, good Cassio!

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: This is the fruit of whoring. Prithee, Emilia,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Go know of Cassio where he supp’d to-night.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: To Bianca. What, do you shake at that?

lines.speaker: BIANCA
lines.text: He supp’d at my house; but I therefore shake not.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: O, did he so? I charge you, go with me.

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Fie, fie upon thee, strumpet!

lines.speaker: BIANCA
lines.text: I am no strumpet; but of life as honest

lines.speaker: BIANCA
lines.text: As you that thus abuse me.

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: As I! foh! fie upon thee!

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Kind gentlemen, let’s go see poor Cassio dress’d.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Come, mistress, you must tell’s another tale.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Emilia run you to the citadel,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: And tell my lord and lady what hath happ’d.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Will you go on? I pray. This is the night

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: That either makes me or fordoes me quite.

glosses.definition: a projecting frame of a shop; a hiding place
pimple — Iago's contemptuous term for Roderigo
a garter pulled off for a bandage
worthless person — Iago's slur over Roderigo's body

annotations.title: Night action and the unraveling of Iago's plot
annotations.body:
The ambush on Cassio is Iago's first major improvisation since the handkerchief. It is also his first real miscalculation. He expects Cassio dead; instead Cassio is wounded and Roderigo is dispatched by Iago himself to cover the failure. The night scene is murky — 'make me a light', 'what are you there?' — and Shakespeare uses the confusion to show Iago losing control of his own scheme.

Structurally the play has been a pyramid of Iago's mastery; from here it is his descent. He has authored too many stories at once and can no longer reconcile them. By the time he enters Desdemona's bedchamber in the next scene, his plot has begun to consume him along with his victims.
sources: Philip Edwards, *Shakespeare and the Confines of Art* (1968)

trials.prompt: What is Iago's plan at the start of the scene?
options: To kill Othello and seize Cyprus
To have Roderigo kill Cassio, gaining him as loyal ally while removing a witness
To poison Desdemona in her sleep
To flee Cyprus disguised as a merchant

trials.prompt: Why does Iago ultimately kill Roderigo himself?
options: Roderigo has betrayed him to Lodovico
Roderigo wounded Cassio but failed to kill him — Iago silences the man who knows the plot
Roderigo has stolen the remaining jewels
Roderigo has refused to pay the agreed-upon sum

trials.prompt: Why does Shakespeare set this scene at night in a dark street?
options: Because Venice and Cyprus had no streetlights
The darkness mirrors Iago's loss of control — his scheme becomes visually as confused as it is strategically
It was easier to stage in the Globe Theatre at sunset
Night scenes were cheaper to perform
